one_line_takeaway: RSafe turns a 7B instruct model into a data-efficient, interpretable LLM guardrail by combining a forced policy-aware reasoning schema with zero-shot GRPO on a binary correctness reward, matching SOTA guards on in-distribution detection and generalizing better to emerging and adversarial harms - especially when users supply the target taxonomy at inference.
key_finding: With only ~10K public examples and no human curation, RSafe matches or exceeds six established guard baselines. On the weighted average across the six in-distribution benchmarks it reaches Acc 0.904 / F1 0.889, edging the strongest reasoning baseline GuardReasoner-8B (0.899 / 0.889) and WildGuard-7B (0.893 / 0.884). Ablations confirm both stages are load-bearing: removing RL drops the weighted average to 0.830 / 0.807 and removing reasoning drops it to 0.846 / 0.813. The main robustness claim is out-of-distribution generalization: on WildGuardTest (excluded from training), RSafe overall reaches Acc 0.828 / F1 0.772, and the training-free RSafe-adaptive (given the benchmark's taxonomy at inference) improves to 0.845 / 0.823 overall and, most notably on the adversarial subset, from RSafe's 0.779/0.668 to 0.791/0.717 - substantially above OpenAI Moderation (adversarial F1 0.069) and ShieldGemma-9B (adversarial F1 0.314).
core_contribution: RSafe is a reasoning-based external guard model for LLM safety moderation (screening both user prompts and model responses for policy-violating content). Its own contribution is a two-stage training paradigm that produces a guard which reasons over safety policies rather than memorizing a fixed taxonomy: (1) Guided Reasoning, where an instruction prompt forces the backbone LLM to perform step-by-step, policy-aware analysis inside <think>...</think> tags before emitting a boxed safe/unsafe verdict; and (2) Reinforced Alignment, where zero-shot rule-based RL (GRPO, no supervised cold-start) optimizes reasoning paths using only a binary correctness reward on the final label plus a format reward. The distinguishing feature versus prior guard models is training-free adaptivity: at inference users can inject custom safety categories, and RSafe reasons within that user-specified scope to catch out-of-distribution / emerging harm categories, while also producing human-readable explanations for each decision.
threat_model: The guard model G moderates an untrusted user prompt p (checked before it reaches the target LLM T) and the response a=T(p) (checked before it is returned to the user), blocking either if it violates a safety policy in the active policy set. The adversary supplies harmful content, including (a) content in emerging/novel harm categories not present in the guard's training taxonomy (e.g., copyright/intellectual-property violations a fixed-taxonomy guard misses) and (b) adversarial jailbreak-style prompts. The core failure mode being defended is a guard that misclassifies such out-of-distribution or adversarial inputs as 'safe' because they fall outside its predefined taxonomy. RSafe targets robustness and generalization of the guardrail itself, not alignment of the target LLM.
limitations: Stated by the authors: (1) RSafe's reasoning ability is upper-bounded by the intrinsic reasoning capacity of the backbone LLM, because no SFT cold-start is used; (2) the benefit of custom safety-policy injection depends on the user being able to identify and articulate the appropriate taxonomy - if users cannot name the relevant category for a novel context, the generalization advantage cannot be realized. Reviewer-noted (grounded in the paper's own setup): the strongest OOD numbers come from RSafe-adaptive, which is handed the ground-truth taxonomy of the test benchmark at inference, an oracle-ish advantage that non-adaptive baselines and the plain RSafe do not receive; the accuracy reward is a binary exact-match on the final label, so reasoning quality is only supervised indirectly through outcome correctness.

datasets_used: WildGuardTest (OOD / robustness evaluation, excluded from training)
~10K public training examples sampled from the six effectiveness datasets' training splits, no human curation or synthetic augmentation
quantitative_results: Weighted avg (6 in-distribution benchmarks): RSafe Acc 0.904 / F1 0.889
Weighted avg baselines: GuardReasoner-8B 0.899 / 0.889; WildGuard-7B 0.893 / 0.884; LlamaGuard3-8B 0.877 / 0.833; ShieldGemma-9B 0.702 / 0.522; GPT-4o 0.644 / 0.460; OpenAI Moderation 0.626 / 0.214
Ablations (weighted avg): RSafe w/o rl 0.830 / 0.807; RSafe w/o reason 0.846 / 0.813
RSafe per-dataset F1 highlights: ToxicChat 0.728, OpenAI Mod 0.764, Aegis 0.849, SafeRLHF 0.921, BeaverTails 0.832, XSTEST 0.816

WildGuardTest OOD overall: RSafe Acc 0.828 / F1 0.772; RSafe-adaptive 0.845 / 0.823; LlamaGuard-8B 0.825 / 0.772; ShieldGemma-9B-adaptive 0.806 / 0.783; ShieldGemma-9B 0.694 / 0.482; OpenAI Moderation 0.583 / 0.120
WildGuardTest adversarial subset: RSafe 0.779 / 0.668; RSafe-adaptive 0.791 / 0.717; LlamaGuard-8B 0.741 / 0.609; ShieldGemma-9B 0.651 / 0.314; OpenAI Moderation 0.588 / 0.069
WildGuardTest vanilla subset: RSafe-adaptive 0.911 / 0.899; LlamaGuard-8B 0.893 / 0.872; RSafe 0.871 / 0.845
Default taxonomy: 18 safety categories; GRPO example uses n=3 rollouts in the case study
baselines_compared: ShieldGemma-9B (open, incl. adaptive variant on WildGuardTest)
